title: The Yule Guest
content:
혻혻혻혻And Yanna by the yule log
혻혻혻혻Sat in the empty hall,
혻혻혻혻And watched the goblin firelight
혻혻혻혻Caper upon the wall:

혻혻혻혻The goblins of the hearthstone,
혻혻혻혻Who teach the wind to sing,
혻혻혻혻Who dance the frozen yule away
혻혻혻혻And usher back the spring;

혻혻혻혻The goblins of the Northland,
혻혻혻혻Who teach the gulls to scream,
혻혻혻혻Who dance the autumn into dust,
혻혻혻혻The ages into dream.

혻혻혻혻Like the tall corn was Yanna,
혻혻혻혻Bending and smooth and fair,--
혻혻혻혻His Yanna of the sea-gray eyes
혻혻혻혻And harvest-yellow hair.

혻혻혻혻Child of the low-voiced people
혻혻혻혻Who dwell among the hills,
혻혻혻혻She had the lonely calm and poise
혻혻혻혻Of life that waits and wills.

혻혻혻혻Only to-night a little
혻혻혻혻With grave regard she smiled,
혻혻혻혻Remembering the morn she woke
혻혻혻혻And ceased to be a child.

혻혻혻혻Outside, the ghostly rampikes,
혻혻혻혻Those armies of the moon,
혻혻혻혻Stood while the ranks of stars drew on
혻혻혻혻To that more spacious noon,--

혻혻혻혻While over them in silence
혻혻혻혻Waved on the dusk afar
혻혻혻혻The gold flags of the Northern light
혻혻혻혻Streaming with ancient war.

혻혻혻혻And when below the headland
혻혻혻혻The riders of the foam
혻혻혻혻Up from the misty border rode
혻혻혻혻The wild gray horses home,

혻혻혻혻And woke the wintry mountains
혻혻혻혻With thunder on the shore,
혻혻혻혻Out of the night there came a weird
혻혻혻혻And cried at Yanna's door.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻They buried me away
혻혻혻혻In the blue fathoms of the deep,
혻혻혻혻Beyond the outer bay.

혻혻혻혻"But in the yule, O Yanna,
혻혻혻혻Up from the round dim sea
혻혻혻혻And reeling dungeons of the fog,
혻혻혻혻I am come back to thee!"

혻혻혻혻The wind slept in the forest,
혻혻혻혻The moon was white and high,
혻혻혻혻Only the shifting snow awoke
혻혻혻혻To hear the yule guest cry.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Yanna, Yanna,
혻혻혻혻Be quick and let me in!
혻혻혻혻For bitter is the trackless way
혻혻혻혻And far that I have been!"

혻혻혻혻Then Yanna by the yule log
혻혻혻혻Starts from her dream to hear
혻혻혻혻A voice that bids her brooding heart
혻혻혻혻Shudder with joy and fear.

혻혻혻혻The wind is up a moment
혻혻혻혻And whistles at the eaves,
혻혻혻혻And in his troubled iron dream
혻혻혻혻The ocean moans and heaves.

혻혻혻혻She trembles at the door-lock
혻혻혻혻That he is come again,
혻혻혻혻And frees the wooden bolt for one
혻혻혻혻No barrier could detain.

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, bonny Garvin,
혻혻혻혻So late, so late you come!"
혻혻혻혻The yule log crumbles down and throws
혻혻혻혻Strange figures on the gloom;

혻혻혻혻But in the moonlight pouring
혻혻혻혻Through the half-open door
혻혻혻혻Stands the gray guest of yule and casts
혻혻혻혻No shadow on the floor.

혻혻혻혻The change that is upon him
혻혻혻혻She knows not in her haste;
혻혻혻혻About him her strong arms with glad
혻혻혻혻Impetuous tears are laced.

혻혻혻혻She's led him to the fireside,
혻혻혻혻And set the wide oak chair,
혻혻혻혻And with her warm hands brushed away
혻혻혻혻The sea-rime from his hair.

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, I have waited,--
혻혻혻혻Have watched the red sun sink,
혻혻혻혻And clouds of sail come flocking in
혻혻혻혻Over the world's gray brink,

혻혻혻혻"With stories of encounter
혻혻혻혻On plank and mast and spar;
혻혻혻혻But never the brave barque I launched
혻혻혻혻And waved across the bar.

혻혻혻혻"How come you so unsignalled,
혻혻혻혻When I have watched so well?
혻혻혻혻Where rides the Adrianna
혻혻혻혻With my name on boat and bell?"

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, golden Yanna,
혻혻혻혻The Adrianna lies
혻혻혻혻With the sea dredging through her ports,
혻혻혻혻The white sand through her eyes.

혻혻혻혻"And strange unearthly creatures
혻혻혻혻Make marvel of her hull,
혻혻혻혻Where far below the gulfs of storm
혻혻혻혻There is eternal lull.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻This midnight I am here,
혻혻혻혻Because one night of all my life
혻혻혻혻At yule tide of the year,

혻혻혻혻"With the stars white in heaven,
혻혻혻혻And peace upon the sea,
혻혻혻혻With all my world in your white arms
혻혻혻혻You gave yourself to me.

혻혻혻혻"For that one night, my Yanna,
혻혻혻혻Within the dying year,
혻혻혻혻Was it not well to love, and now
혻혻혻혻Can it be well to fear?"

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, there is heartache
혻혻혻혻In tales that are half told;
혻혻혻혻But ah, thy cheek is pale to-night,
혻혻혻혻And thy poor hands are cold!

혻혻혻혻"Tell me the course, the voyage,
혻혻혻혻The ports, and the new stars;
혻혻혻혻Did the long rollers make green surf
혻혻혻혻On the white reefs and bars?"

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻Though easily I found
혻혻혻혻The set of those uncharted tides
혻혻혻혻In seas no line could sound,

혻혻혻혻"And made without a pilot
혻혻혻혻The port without a light,
혻혻혻혻No log keeps tally of the knots
혻혻혻혻That I have sailed to-night.

혻혻혻혻"It fell about mid-April;
혻혻혻혻The Trades were holding free;
혻혻혻혻We drove her till the scuppers hissed
혻혻혻혻And buried in the lee.

혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻Loose hands and let me go!
혻혻혻혻The night grows red along the East,
혻혻혻혻And in the shifting snow

혻혻혻혻"I hear my shipmates calling,
혻혻혻혻Sent out to search for me
혻혻혻혻In the pale lands beneath the moon
혻혻혻혻Along the troubling sea."

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, bonny Garvin,
혻혻혻혻What is the booming sound
혻혻혻혻Of canvas, and the piping shrill,
혻혻혻혻As when a ship comes round?"

혻혻혻혻"It is the shadow boatswain
혻혻혻혻Piping his hands to bend
혻혻혻혻The looming sails on giant yards
혻혻혻혻Aboard the Nomansfriend.

혻혻혻혻"She sails for Sunken Harbor
혻혻혻혻And ports of yester year;
혻혻혻혻The tern are shrilling in the lift,
혻혻혻혻The low wind-gates are clear.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻The little while is done.
혻혻혻혻Thou wilt behold the brightening sea
혻혻혻혻Freshen before the sun,

혻혻혻혻"And many a morning redden
혻혻혻혻The dark hill slopes of pine;
혻혻혻혻But I must sail hull-down to-night
혻혻혻혻Below the gray sea-line.

혻혻혻혻"I shall not hear the snowbirds
혻혻혻혻Their morning litany,
혻혻혻혻For when the dawn comes over dale
혻혻혻혻I must put out to sea."

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, bonny Garvin,
혻혻혻혻To have thee as I will,
혻혻혻혻I would that never more on earth
혻혻혻혻The dawn came over hill."

혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 *

혻혻혻혻Then on the snowy pillow,
혻혻혻혻Her hair about her face,
혻혻혻혻He laid her in the quiet room,
혻혻혻혻And wiped away all trace

혻혻혻혻Of tears from the poor eyelids
혻혻혻혻That were so sad for him,
혻혻혻혻And soothed her into sleep at last
혻혻혻혻As the great stars grew dim.

혻혻혻혻Tender as April twilight
혻혻혻혻He sang, and the song grew
혻혻혻혻Vague as the dreams which roam about
혻혻혻혻This world of dust and dew:

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻Dear Love, look forth to sea
혻혻혻혻And all year long until the yule,
혻혻혻혻Dear Heart, keep watch for me!

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻I hear the calling sea,
혻혻혻혻And the folk telling tales among
혻혻혻혻The hills where I would be.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻Over the hills of sea
혻혻혻혻The wind calls and the morning comes,
혻혻혻혻And I must forth from thee.

혻혻혻혻"But Yanna, Adrianna,
혻혻혻혻Keep watch above the sea;
혻혻혻혻And when the weary time is o'er,
혻혻혻혻Dear Life, come back to me!"

혻혻혻혻"O Garvin, bonny Garvin--"
혻혻혻혻She murmurs in her dream,
혻혻혻혻And smiles a moment in her sleep
혻혻혻혻To hear the white gulls scream.

혻혻혻혻Then with the storm foreboding
혻혻혻혻Far in the dim gray South,
혻혻혻혻He kissed her not upon the cheek
혻혻혻혻Nor on the burning mouth,

혻혻혻혻But once above the forehead
혻혻혻혻Before he turned away;
혻혻혻혻And ere the morning light stole in,
혻혻혻혻That golden lock was gray.

혻혻혻혻"O Yanna, Adrianna--"
혻혻혻혻The wind moans to the sea;
혻혻혻혻And down the sluices of the dawn
혻혻혻혻A shadow drifts alee.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bliss Carman (William), titled "The Yule Guest", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
